skill.name: metabolomics-workbench-database
skill.description: Access NIH Metabolomics Workbench (4,200+ studies) via REST API. Query metabolites, RefMet nomenclature, MS/NMR data, m/z search, study metadata for metabolomics and biomarker discovery.
skill.summary: A documentation-first skill providing reference material and code examples for querying the NIH Metabolomics Workbench public REST API for metabolomics research.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 348 static analysis findings are false positives originating from documentation code examples in markdown files. This skill package contains no executable scripts - only reference documentation and API usage examples demonstrating calls to the legitimate NIH Metabolomics Workbench public REST API. Weak cryptographic algorithm alerts stem from URL path segments being misidentified, while shell execution alerts are curl commands in markdown code blocks. The package passed all audit gates including security and is rated Production Ready with a score of 86/100.

content.user_title: Query NIH Metabolomics Workbench for Metabolomics Research
content.value_statement: This skill gives you direct access to over 4,200 metabolomics studies from the NIH Metabolomics Workbench through a documented REST API workflow. It enables you to search metabolites, standardize names with RefMet, perform m/z searches, and retrieve study data for biomarker discovery and metabolomics research.
actual_capabilities: Query metabolite structures and data by multiple identifiers including PubChem CID, InChI Key, KEGG ID, and HMDB ID
Access study metadata and experimental results from over 4,200 metabolomics studies
Standardize metabolite names using the RefMet nomenclature database for cross-study consistency
Perform mass spectrometry m/z searches with configurable ion adduct and tolerance parameters
Filter studies by analysis method, species, sample source, and disease using the MetStat interface
Retrieve gene and protein information related to metabolic pathways and metabolite metabolism
limitations: Cannot perform statistical analysis or data processing on retrieved data - this is a data access skill only
Requires internet access to reach the Metabolomics Workbench REST API at metabolomicsworkbench.org
Limited to publicly available studies (3,790+ out of 4,200+ total studies)
Does not include a packaged script for batch processing or large-scale automated data extraction

use_cases.title: Metabolite Identification from Mass Spectrometry Data
use_cases.description: Search compounds by m/z values with specific ion adducts and tolerance levels to identify unknown metabolites from LC-MS or GC-MS experiments.
use_cases.target_user: Mass spectrometry researchers and metabolomics scientists

use_cases.title: Standardizing Metabolite Names Across Studies
use_cases.description: Convert non-standard metabolite names to consistent RefMet nomenclature for cross-study comparison and meta-analysis in multi-dataset projects.
use_cases.target_user: Bioinformaticians and data analysts working with multi-study datasets

use_cases.title: Exploring Disease-Specific Metabolomics Studies
use_cases.description: Filter the Metabolomics Workbench by disease, species, and analysis method to find relevant studies for biomarker discovery and literature review.
use_cases.target_user: Medical researchers and clinical scientists investigating disease biomarkers

prompt_templates.title: Basic Metabolite Search
prompt_templates.prompt: Search the Metabolomics Workbench for the metabolite glucose. Retrieve its compound information including molecular formula, exact mass, and database cross-references.
prompt_templates.scenario: Looking up basic metabolite information by common name

prompt_templates.title: Finding Studies for a Metabolite
prompt_templates.prompt: Find all studies in the Metabolomics Workbench that contain measurements for the metabolite Tyrosine. List the study IDs, titles, and analysis methods used.
prompt_templates.scenario: Discovering which studies have measured a specific metabolite of interest

prompt_templates.title: m/z Based Compound Identification
prompt_templates.prompt: Search the Metabolomics Workbench for compounds matching m/z value 180.06 with M+H adduct and 0.5 Da tolerance. Describe the candidate compounds found and their database identifiers.
prompt_templates.scenario: Identifying unknown compounds from mass spectrometry data

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Criteria Study Search
prompt_templates.prompt: Search the Metabolomics Workbench for human blood studies related to diabetes using LC-MS with positive ionization mode. Retrieve the study summaries and experimental factors for review.
prompt_templates.scenario: Finding studies matching specific experimental conditions and disease criteria

output_examples.input: Search for metabolite glucose in the Metabolomics Workbench.
output_examples.output: Glucose (C6H12O6) has an exact mass of 180.0634 Da. It is classified as an Organic compound, specifically a Carbohydrate under Monosaccharides. Cross-references include PubChem CID 5793, HMDB HMDB0000122, and KEGG C00031.

output_examples.input: Find all studies containing the metabolite Tyrosine.
output_examples.output: Tyrosine is measured in 47 studies. Study ST000001 (Diabetes in Human Plasma) uses LC-MS with positive ionization. Study ST000045 (Rat Liver Metabolomics) uses GC-MS. Each study includes experimental data with metabolite concentrations and sample metadata.

output_examples.input: What studies are available for human blood and diabetes?
output_examples.output: Found 12 studies matching Human blood and diabetes with LC-MS analysis. Studies include ST000123 (Type 2 Diabetes Biomarkers, 150 patients) and ST000456 (Metabolic Profiling of Diabetic Nephropathy). Available data includes raw metabolite measurements, clinical metadata, and analysis parameters.

best_practices: Always standardize metabolite names through RefMet before searching studies to ensure consistent nomenclature across different data sources.
Set appropriate mass tolerance values based on your instrument resolution - use 0.5 Da for low-resolution MS and 0.01 Da for high-resolution MS.
Use the correct ion adduct type matching your analytical method to get accurate m/z search results (M+H for positive mode ESI, M-H for negative mode).
anti_patterns: Do not use non-standard metabolite names for study searches without first converting them through RefMet, as this will produce incomplete results.
Do not ignore pagination in responses - large result sets may be truncated and require iterative fetching for complete data.
Do not assume all 4,200 studies are publicly accessible - verify study availability before designing your analysis workflow.

faq.question: What is the Metabolomics Workbench?
faq.answer: It is the primary repository for metabolomics research data, sponsored by the NIH Common Fund and hosted at the University of California, San Diego (UCSD).

faq.question: How many studies are available?
faq.answer: Over 4,200 processed studies are available in total, with 3,790 or more publicly accessible without restrictions.

faq.question: What is RefMet?
faq.answer: RefMet is a standardized metabolite nomenclature system that provides consistent naming across different data sources at four levels of structural resolution.

faq.question: What output formats does the API support?
faq.answer: The API supports JSON for programmatic access and TXT for human-readable tab-separated output. JSON is the default format.

faq.question: Do I need an API key to access this data?
faq.answer: No, the Metabolomics Workbench REST API is publicly accessible without authentication for most endpoints and standard use cases.

faq.question: What analytical platforms are covered?
faq.answer: The database includes data from GC-MS, LC-MS, NMR, ICP-MS, and other analytical platforms across diverse biological species.
